articles.title: Europe's deadly heat wave scorches east, Slovakia hits record
articles.description: Europe's most severe heat wave on record set new temperature records in eastern parts of the continent on Monday and forced Ukraine to order power cuts to cope.

articles.title: How PFAS chain length influences environmental fate and water treatment
articles.description: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), often referred to as "forever chemicals," are among the most persistent contaminants found in water systems worldwide. Their strong carbon-fluorine bonds make them highly resistant to degradation, allowing them to remain in the environment for long periods. While PFAS are often treated as a single group of pollutants, growing evidence suggests that differences in their molecular structure can significantly influence both their environmental behavior and their response to treatment technologies.

articles.title: School performance linked to youth criminal justice
articles.description: Students whose performance at school declines relative to their peers are at a higher risk of contact with the criminal justice system, a new study by King's College London has found.

articles.title: South Korea’s Qcells tests tandem solar modules for 2029 launch
articles.description: Hanwha Qcells will test commercial-scale tandem solar modules with a government-backed consortium as it targets commercialization in 2029.

articles.title: Tailored supplier strategies could cut emissions better than one-size-fits-all procurement
articles.description: Companies hoping to reduce the environmental impact of their purchasing should tailor relationships with suppliers to different types of products rather than relying on a single procurement strategy, according to research published in the International Journal of Procurement Management.

articles.title: Scientists discover two superconductors with method that could find thousands more
articles.description: Smart computing identifies two new superconductors, accelerating the search for room-temperature materials.

articles.title: AI tool reliably predicts the flame resistance of new materials
articles.description: Researchers at IMDEA Materials Institute have developed an artificial intelligence (AI)-based strategy to predict and assess the fire resistance of epoxy resins, one of the most widely used polymers in industry.

articles.title: Unexpected pathway turns water and CO2 into climate‐neutral methane on nickel–zirconia
articles.description: Natural gas still plays an important role in many industrial sectors, but it is a climate-damaging fossil fuel. TU Wien and the University of Innsbruck have now discovered an unexpected reaction pathway that makes it possible to synthesize natural gas, or methane (CH4), using CO2 that was previously captured from exhaust gas streams or directly from the air. In this way, methane can become climate-neutral overall.

articles.title: Meet NASAs new prototype rover for moon & mars
articles.description: Meet ERNEST, NASA's prototype rover designed to test technologies that could support future exploration of the Moon and Mars.

articles.title: NASA aims to save a sinking space telescope with a rendezvous in orbit
articles.description: NASA on Tuesday plans to launch a mission to save its Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory. A spacecraft is expected to push the telescope into a stable orbit so it doesn’t fall into the atmosphere.

articles.title: Madonna Issues Explosive Take on AI
articles.description: Sorry, AI "artists." The post Madonna Issues Explosive Take on AI appeared first on Futurism .

articles.title: Faster tests reveal six fluoropolymer microplastics, including four rarely tracked types
articles.description: Scientists around the world have been searching food, water and other environmental media for microplastics and for per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS). But microfluoroplastics (MFPs), the intersection between these two fields, have received much less attention.

articles.title: Robotic Servicing Spacecraft Will Soon Blast into Orbit, to Try to Save a 1.5-Ton NASA Satellite From Reentry
articles.description: Learn more about the Swift satellite, why it's slowly being dragged back to Earth, and how LINK will help keep it in space.

articles.title: Rare inner ear cells point to regenerative hearing treatments
articles.description: A study by a team of researchers from the Gray Faculty of Medical and Health Sciences at Tel Aviv University offers new hope to millions of people with irreversible hearing loss. The researchers identified a unique biological mechanism that could, in the future, enable the regeneration of sensory hair cells in the inner ear—a process previously thought impossible in humans.

articles.title: China completes full-performance tests of world’s largest superconducting magnet
articles.description: China tests the world's largest superconducting fusion magnet, advancing next-generation reactor technology.

articles.title: Disorder creates direction-dependent optics in compound semiconductors
articles.description: An international research team has demonstrated that the intrinsic disorder of the compound semiconductor CuInSnS4 can be exploited to influence its optical properties. While the atomic vibrations also sense the local disorder, their response is averaged over many different local environments and therefore appears isotropic, as expected for a cubic crystal.

articles.title: New cellular model for rare and deadly melanomas enables study of immunotherapy resistance
articles.description: A research team at the University of Turku in Finland has developed a reliable laboratory model to study BAP1-deficient melanomas, which are a rare type of melanoma that evade the immune system once they have metastasized and are universally resistant to current state-of-the-art immunotherapies. The tool could change how therapies are developed for aggressive melanomas that currently have almost no effective treatment options.

articles.title: World's Most Powerful Collider Shuts Down for a Smashing Upgrade
articles.description: Europe's CERN physics research center bids 'Farewell' to the Large Hadron Collider, but it's actually more like 'See You Later, Accelerator!' The new, improved High-Luminosity LHC is due to make its debut in 2030.

articles.title: ESA’s ExoMars Trace Gas Orbiter Has Yet to Detect Methane On Mars
articles.description: After more than eight years of searching and with instruments designed to detect it, the European Space Agency’s (ESA) Mars Trace Gas Orbiter has yet to find methane in the red planet’s atmosphere.

articles.title: Deep-sea extremophile yields protein that forms super stable biofilm
articles.description: Scientists discovered a protein secreted by a deep-sea extremophile—an organism adapted to extreme environmental conditions—that self-assembles into a biofilm and is highly stable, boosting its potential for biomedical applications.

articles.title: CERN shutters the Large Hadron Collider for a major transformation
articles.description: The High-Luminosity LHC, planned to switch on in 2030, could help physicists unravel mysteries about the Higgs boson, dark matter and more.

articles.title: As Oceans Warm, UCI Researchers Find Clues to Which Species Shifts Could Matter Most
articles.description: First author Ryan Beshai, left, and Professor Cascade Sorte, right, led a new study that offers a framework for predicting how climate-driven species shifts may affect marine ecosystems. Irvine, Calif., [...]

articles.title: Man uses a Game Boy Camera to photograph Jupiter
articles.description: With a big boost from Mount Wilson Observatory’s powerful Hooker Telescope. The post Man uses a Game Boy Camera to photograph Jupiter appeared first on Popular Science .

articles.title: Study reveals how leukemia cells invade lungs and cause breathing problems
articles.description: Led by NYU Langone Health researchers, a new study shows how blood cancer cells enter the lungs, damage tissue, and cause severe breathing problems.

articles.title: America 250: America’s space journey from Apollo to Artemis
articles.description: America’s push to reach the moon transformed science, technology and exploration. More than 50 years after Apollo 11, the next era of spaceflight is taking shape.

articles.title: Extreme heat is setting in for July 4. Here’s what to know
articles.description: A prolonged, intense heat wave will make temperatures feel as hot as 115 degrees Fahrenheit in the eastern U.S. this week

articles.title: US backs next-gen chips to speed geothermal drilling and boost energy security
articles.description: I-Pulse wins $250M CHIPS award to develop silicon carbide semiconductors for geothermal drilling and defense

articles.title: New superconductors identified, unlocking process that could yield thousands more
articles.description: An international team of quantum researchers has shown how machine learning can be used to filter a practically infinite number of possible material combinations to identify candidates for superconductivity. Thanks to the breakthrough, new superconductors can now be found much faster, says Aalto University Professor Päivi Törmä, who leads the SuperC consortium behind the research.

articles.title: New Delhi announces EV policy to combat air pollution
articles.description: New Delhi authorities announced a new policy Monday aimed at curbing pollution in the city, including incentives to get residents to scrap old vehicles for electric ones.

articles.title: Into the bog: Stepping into the wild heart of the Baltics
articles.description: A walk in the foggy, soggy wetlands of Estonia is an experience that CNN’s Richard Quest won’t soon forget.

articles.title: Age bias discovered in AI
articles.description: Do responses generated by artificial intelligence systems such as ChatGPT reflect social prejudice? A KAIST research team has quantitatively analyzed and identified age-related stereotypes embedded in the responses of generative artificial intelligence. The study sheds light on the potential impact of hidden AI biases on social perceptions and suggests directions for the development of more inclusive AI.

articles.title: Mars orbiter watches Perseverance rover cross the marathon finish line | Space photo of the day for June 29...
articles.description: With the speed at which the rover completed this marathon, and how well it continues to explore on Mars, who knows, maybe Perseverance will be the first to m...

articles.title: STAT+: Talawar bets bispecific approach can shake up eczema market
articles.description: Moderna still bullish on mRNA, Ipsen's acquisition of Kartos, and more biotech news from The Readout

articles.title: The Rise of Space AI Might Explain the Fermi Paradox
articles.description: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is continuing to have a disruptive impact on ever more parts of humanity. But what does it mean in the long run? A new paper, available in pre-print on arXiv from Austrian researcher Sergey Ivliev, extrapolates what the wide scale adoption of AI means for the future of humanity in space - and in particular what it means for the ultimate question of whether we’re truly alone in the galaxy or not.

articles.title: Keep an eye out for tonight's Strawberry Moon
articles.description: The Strawberry Moon is set to rise in UK skies tonight, marking the first full Moon of the summer season. It will reach its peak in the early hours of Tuesday, 30 June, according to the Royal Observatory Greenwich.

articles.title: Chiltern Vital Group and Cambridge Atomworks (2024) Ltd sign Letter of Intent to facilitate research and development into leading edge nuclear energy generation technology at the Berkeley Green Science and Technology Park, Gloucestershire
articles.description: BERKELEY, United Kingdom, June 29, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Chiltern Vital Group (CVG) and Cambridge Atomworks are pleased to announce the signing of a letter of intent to facilitate research and development into Cambridge Atomworks’ ODIN nuclear micro-reactor on the Berkeley Green Science and Technology Park. Chiltern Vital Berkeley (CVB), the Group company that owns and [...]

articles.title: Photos: New TR02 robotic hand transitions objects from fingertips to palm like humans do
articles.description: Check latest photos on New Innovations, Robots, Engineering, Science, Space and more on Interesting Engineering

articles.title: More than half of social media child safety features aren’t working as advertised, new research finds
articles.description: Researchers at the Cybersafety Research Center found that more than half of social media child safety protections don’t work as advertised.

articles.title: World’s largest particle accelerator shuts down to undergo major 4-year upgrade
articles.description: The Large Hadron Collider is scheduled to shut down today for a $1.5 billion upgrade expected to be completed in four years' time.

articles.title: Where is best to see the Strawberry Moon tonight?
articles.description: The first full Moon of the summer will be visible tonight, reaching its peak in the early hours of Tuesday.

articles.title: Megalodon fossil confirms true size of massive shark
Malaysia to host AMMSTI-23 in June 2027
articles.description: KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is set to host the 23rd Asean Ministerial Meeting on Science, Technology and Innovation (AMMSTI-23) in June 2027.

articles.title: STAT+: Moderna co-founder Kenneth Chien on the future of mRNA — and Moderna
articles.description: In a Q&A, Kenneth Chien, no longer with Moderna, predicts the company's mRNA cancer vaccine will be transformative for biotech.

articles.title: STAT+: Investor Clive Meanwell on designing biotechs for population health, and AI’s role as a catalyst
articles.description: Clive Meanwell, chairman of investment firm Population Health Partners, was an AI skeptic until recently. Now, he thinks it's a catalyst.

articles.title: Opinion: STAT+: The U.S.-China biotech crackdown may hurt the scientists America needs the most
articles.description: “There is a difference between a policy based on evidence and a broad legislative response that targets China-origin entities as inherently suspicious,” writes Brian Yang.

articles.title: The Big Changes Coming to the Times/Siena Poll
articles.description: The goal is to make the survey more deeply representative of the population.

articles.title: The Black Holes That Burp Years After They Eat
articles.description: When a star strays too close to a supermassive black hole, it is torn apart in a brief, brilliant flare, and astronomers long assumed that was the end of the story. It isn't. Using the Very Large Array to follow dozens of these stellar killings, a team has discovered that many black holes "burp" months or even years later, belching out streams of radio light as they fling part of their meal back into space. These delayed flares let astronomers watch a black hole's appetite change in real time, and reveal that even our Galaxy's quietest looking giants are messier, and far more active, than anyone realised.

articles.title: An Island of Calm at the Violent Heart of the Galaxy
articles.description: The centre of the Milky Way is one of the most violent places in the Galaxy, a churning storm of gas moving faster than sound, and about the last spot you would expect a star to be born. Yet astronomers mapping that chaos with the ALMA telescope have stumbled on a hidden pocket of calm, where the gas slows, settles and quietly begins gathering itself into the seeds of new stars. The find suggests stars may take their first steps the same way everywhere, even here, and that our own Sun was likely born from just such an island of stillness, billions of years ago.

articles.title: Two Planets Lighter Than Candy Floss
articles.description: Astronomers have found two of the lightest worlds ever discovered, a pair of giant planets so wispy that, gram for gram, they are less dense than candy floss. Each is roughly the size of Jupiter yet holds almost nothing inside and the two circle the same distant star as siblings, locked in a gravitational dance that sees them tug one another off schedule as they orbit. It was observations from the depths of the Antarctic winter, that let astronomers weigh them and uncover just how astonishingly insubstantial they are. Now they want to know just how a planet ends up barely heavier than air at all.

articles.title: Two hospitals in Japan to conduct pig-to-human kidney transplant clinical trials in 2028
articles.description: The startup spun out of Meiji University said it would aim to obtain production and marketing authorization after confirming their safety through the trials.
